Law of Large Numbers

Jacob Bernoulli

Asymmetry lens

Decision lens: Law of Large Numbers.

statisticsdistribution

Stoic Virtue Ethics

Marcus Aurelius · 170 CE

Ethics lens

Focusing on virtue and what is within your control as the path to well-being and wisdom.

PhilosophyEthicsPsychology

Inversion Thinking

Carl Jacobi · 1841

Risk lens

Solving problems by inverting them—asking "what would cause failure?" rather than "how do I succeed?"

Problem-SolvingStrategyRisk Management

Prisoner's Dilemma

Merrill Flood & Melvin Dresher · 1950

Coordination lens

A game theory model showing how individual incentives can lead to collectively suboptimal outcomes.

Game TheoryCoordinationIncentives

Pareto Principle (80/20)

Vilfredo Pareto · 1906

Asymmetry lens

In many phenomena, roughly 80% of effects come from 20% of causes. Focus on high-leverage factors.

EconomicsOptimizationPrioritization

Economic Moat (Competitive Advantage)

Warren Buffett · 1983

Power lens

Identifying defensible, sustainable competitive advantages that protect against competition.

BusinessStrategyEconomics

Filter Bubble

Eli Pariser · 2011

Asymmetry lens

Algorithmic personalization creates self-reinforcing information silos that limit exposure to diverse perspectives.

TechnologyMediaSociety

Margin of Safety

Benjamin Graham · 1949

Risk lens

Only investing when price is significantly below intrinsic value, building in protection against errors.

InvestingRisk ManagementFinance

Antifragility

Nassim Taleb · 2012

Risk lens

Systems that gain from disorder and volatility, becoming stronger through stress and challenges.

Risk ManagementStrategySystems

Zero-Sum Bias

David Landes · 1969

Incentives lens

Mistakenly viewing situations as zero-sum when they actually offer mutual gains through trade.

EconomicsNegotiationStrategy

Epistemic Humility

Karl Popper · 1945

Ethics lens

Acknowledging the limits of knowledge and the fallibility of expertise, maintaining openness to revision.

PhilosophyScienceDecision-Making

Base Rate Neglect

Tversky & Kahneman · 1973

Feedback lens

Overweighting specific evidence while underweighting background probability rates when making judgments.

StatisticsBiasReasoning

Disconfirmation Bias

Festinger & Karpelowitz · 1961

Feedback lens

Tendency to reject disconfirming evidence more strongly than accepting confirming evidence.

PsychologyBiasReasoning

Dunning-Kruger Effect

Dunning & Kruger · 1999

Feedback lens

Incompetent people overestimate their competence while experts underestimate theirs.

PsychologyBiasOverconfidence

Hyperbolic Discounting

George Ainslie · 1975

Time lens

Disproportionate preference for immediate rewards over future rewards, even when future rewards are larger.

PsychologyEconomicsTime Preference

Ikigai (Purpose & Meaning)

Japanese Philosophy · 1960

Ethics lens

Finding the intersection of passion, talent, service, and compensation creates meaningful, sustainable life.

Life PurposeFulfillmentWork-Life

Metabolic Health Framework

Modern Medicine · 2000

Feedback lens

Health determined by metabolic markers (glucose control, lipid profile, inflammation) more than BMI alone.

HealthMedicineFitness

Trauma-Informed Care

Bessel van der Kolk · 1994

Ethics lens

Recognizing impact of trauma on behavior and physiology, designing interventions that do not re-traumatize.

Mental HealthPsychologyCare

Carrying Capacity (Limits to Growth)

Meadows & Club of Rome · 1972

Feedback lens

Every system has limits. Population and consumption cannot grow indefinitely without system collapse.

EcologySystemsSustainability

Just-World Bias

Melvin Lerner · 1965

Feedback lens

Belief that people get what they deserve and outcomes are fair, leading to victim-blaming.

PsychologyBiasJustice

Identity Salience & In-group Bias

Henri Tajfel · 1970

Power lens

Highlighting group identity makes in-group bias stronger and out-group derogation more likely.

PsychologySociologyIdentity

Attention Residue

Sophie Leroy · 2009

Time lens

Part of attention remains on previous task when switching contexts, reducing effectiveness on new task.

PsychologyProductivityAttention

Pre-Mortem

Gary Klein · 2007

Risk lens

Before project launch, imagine it failed and identify likely causes, surfacing risks not caught by standard planning.

Risk ManagementPlanningStrategy

Planning Fallacy

Kahneman & Tversky · 1979

Time lens

Underestimating time, costs, and risks while overestimating benefits of future actions.

PsychologyBiasPlanning

Golden Circle

Simon Sinek · 2009

Incentives lens

Start with purpose (WHY) before process (HOW) and output (WHAT) to inspire action.

leadershipcommunicationbranding

No models match your current filters.

Socratic Method

High Confidence

Socrates · 399 BCE

What This Model Is

Socratic Method is a structured decision lens used to interpret patterns, incentives, constraints, and trade-offs within a system. It helps decision-makers identify core variables, anticipate consequences, and choose actions aligned with underlying dynamics rather than surface appearances.

Why This Model Exists

To replace assertion and rhetoric with disciplined inquiry that tests whether ideas can survive scrutiny.

Core Logic / Mechanism

  • Ask precise definitions
  • Probe assumptions
  • Seek counterexamples
  • Trace implications

What It Clarifies

  • What you actually mean
  • Which assumptions do the work
  • Where your reasoning breaks

Typical Errors It Prevents

Undefined termsHidden assumptionsOverconfident conclusions

When NOT to Use This Model

When you need rapid execution and the questioner role will stall action (e.g., emergency response).

Historical Application

Plato’s dialogues depict Socrates using questioning to test claims about virtue and justice.

Related Models

Decision Context Matching

Select the characteristics of your decision to identify relevant mental models.

Decision Characteristics

Recommended Models

Select decision characteristics above to see recommended mental models.

Manage imports, videos, activity log and exports

Export Library

Download the full model library as a JSON file. Includes all custom video IDs.

Clear Imported Models

Remove all imported models, keep original 30

JSON Schema Reference

Use this format when creating JSON files for import.

Schema .json 
{
  "id": "unique-id",
  "name": "Model Name",
  "thinker": "Author Name",
  "era": "ancient|medieval|earlymodern
         |industrial|digital|aiera",
  "year": "2024",
  "domain": "politics|science|strategy
         |economics|finance|health
         |technology|culture|law
         |business|environment|identity",
  "lens": "power|risk|incentives|feedback
         |ethics|optionality|time
         |asymmetry|coordination",
  "confidence": "high|medium",
  "description": "One-line summary",
  "tags": ["Tag1"],
  "contextMatch": ["high-stakes"],
  "youtubeId": "dQw4w9WgXcQ",
  "whatItIs": "Short explanation",
  "whyExists": "Historical context",
  "coreMechanism": ["Step 1"],
  "clarifies": ["Clarification"],
  "errorsPrevented": ["Error"],
  "whenNotToUse": "Conditions",
  "historicalExample": "Example",
  "relatedModels": ["model-id"]
}

Field Guide

Required

id, name, thinker, era, year, domain, lens, confidence, description

Recommended

tags, contextMatch, whatItIs, whyExists, coreMechanism, relatedModels

Optional

youtubeId, clarifies, errorsPrevented, whenNotToUse, historicalExample

Batch Import

Wrap multiple models in a JSON array to import them in one go:

[{...model1}, {...model2}]

GitHub Auto-Deploy

When you import models, they get permanently saved to GitHub and Vercel auto-redeploys. Works for all visitors, on every device, forever.

How it works

1

You enter your GitHub token once and save it (stored in your browser only)

2

Upload models in the Import tab as usual

3

Click "Deploy to GitHub" — the app commits the new models to your repo

4

Vercel detects the commit and auto-deploys in ~30 seconds — permanent for everyone

GitHub Personal Access Token

Needs repo scope. Click here to create one →

✓ Token saved

Repository Settings

✓ Config saved

Deploy Current Library

Commits all 224 models to GitHub. Vercel auto-deploys in ~30 seconds.

Methodology & Sources

Selection Criteria

Mental models in this library are selected based on three criteria: historical provenance (documented use by decision-makers), empirical support (evidence from behavioral sciences or historical outcomes), and conceptual clarity (clearly defined mechanisms and boundaries of application).

YouTube Videos

Each model's detail page features a curated YouTube video. To set or change a video, open any model and click the red "Set Video" button. Paste any YouTube URL or video ID. Your changes are saved in your browser's local storage.

Confidence Ratings

  • High confidence: Multiple independent historical sources, empirical validation, and consistent application across domains.
  • Medium confidence: Strong theoretical foundation with some historical support; may have limited empirical testing or domain-specific constraints.

Limitations

This library presents frameworks, not prescriptions. Mental models are tools for structuring thought, not algorithms for optimal decisions. Context matters. Historical effectiveness does not guarantee future applicability.

Primary Sources

Models are drawn from primary historical texts, peer-reviewed research in decision science, and documented practices of institutions with long track records of consequential decision-making.

Mental Model Library — Evidence-Based Decision Intelligence

For educational and research purposes
